topic: teaching Bunjil to track animals on country

turns.content: I have been trying to teach Bunjil to track animals out on country and I am running into a wall. He is keen but he wants to skip straight to finding the animal. Does not want to slow down and read the ground first.
That's pretty typical for kids -- they want the payoff before they've learned the process. How old is Bunjil?

He is 10. Plenty old enough to start learning. I was reading tracks by his age. But the way I was taught was different. My aunties just walked with me and pointed things out as we went. There was no lesson plan. It just happened naturally over years. I am trying to do the same thing but he gets impatient.

The walking-and-pointing method is actually one of the most effective forms of teaching -- it's experiential and contextual. But every kid has a different threshold for patience. What kind of tracks are you starting with?

Wallaby tracks near the billabong. They come down to drink early morning and leave proper clear prints in the mud. I thought that would be a good starting point because the tracks are obvious. But he looks at them and just sees marks in the dirt. He does not see the story.

Seeing the story is a skill that develops over time. One approach that works well with kids his age is to make it a guessing game rather than a lesson. Instead of telling him what the tracks mean, ask him questions. Which direction was the wallaby heading? Was it walking or running? How can you tell? Let him guess, even if he's wrong. The wrong guesses often stick better than the right answers.

That is a good idea. He does like figuring things out for himself. Last week I showed him the difference between a walking track and a running track, how the toes dig in deeper when they push off. He got excited about that for a moment. Then a bird flew past and he was off chasing it.

Ha, that's a 10-year-old for you. But that moment of excitement about the toe marks is something to build on. You could reinforce that lesson by having him make his own tracks -- run in soft ground, then walk, and compare his own footprints. Kids understand their own bodies first. Once he connects his movement to the marks he leaves, he'll start reading animal tracks with the same logic.
Making his own tracks. I like that. He would enjoy it too. Turn it into a game where he has to work out if his sister was walking or running based on her footprints. Tali might even join in for that.

That's a great extension of the idea. Having a sibling involved adds a social element, and if they're trying to fool each other -- walking in a way that looks like running, or vice versa -- they'll both develop a much sharper eye for the details. It becomes play rather than instruction.

Play rather than instruction. That is how it should be. My aunties never sat me down and said today we are learning about goanna tracks. We were just walking and they would stop at a tree and say look at those claw marks, what do you think made those. It was woven into the walking. I have been making it too formal with Bunjil I think.

It sounds like you already have the right model -- you just need to trust it. The walking-beside approach works because the learning is inseparable from the experience. Formal teaching creates a separation between the knowledge and the place, which goes against how this kind of learning is meant to happen. If you relax the structure and just take him out, the teaching moments will come on their own.

You are right. I think I was putting pressure on myself to teach him quickly because school holidays are here and I have him with me most days. Felt like I should use the time. But maybe the better approach is just to walk together and let it happen.

A few weeks of walking together daily will build more than you might expect, even if each day only produces one small observation. Consistency matters more than intensity at this stage. And don't underestimate what he's absorbing passively. He may not seem like he's paying attention, but being out on country with you regularly is laying groundwork.

That is what my grandmother always said. The sitting comes first, the knowing comes after. You do not rush country. Country teaches in its own time. I need to remember that with Bunjil. He will get there.

That's a beautiful way to frame it. And the fact that he's willing to come out with you and he does get excited, even briefly, about things like the toe marks -- that tells you the interest is there. The patience will follow. He's 10. He has time.
He does have time. I will take him out tomorrow morning early. Billabong. Just walking. No plan. See what he notices on his own. If he chases a bird, that is alright too. At least he is out there.
That sounds like a perfect morning. Let him lead sometimes -- you might be surprised by what catches his eye when he's not being directed. Enjoy the walk.
